September 2005 was an unseasonably warm and dry month across the state of Pennsylvania. The unseasonably dry weather began for most in mid August and was mainly an agricultural concern. Nevertheless, several municipalities had to either request or mandate water conservation. In Sellersville (Bucks County), a drought warning was declared. Water use restrictions included the banning of watering lawns, gardens, trees and shrubs other than with a bucket. The borough prohibited the non-essential use of water. In East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), a drought watch was issued and the borough asked for a five percent reduction in water usage. Requests for voluntary reductions in consumption were made by Mount Pocono (Monroe County), Nazareth (Northampton County), Coatesville (Chester County) and Media (Delaware County).     Because of the higher cost for fuel, irrigation costs increased. The heat damaged the leaves of temperature sensitive plants such as lettuce, parsley and cilantro. The lack of rain reduced the size of lettuce, cabbage, leeks and arugula. The sun helped cause blistering of tomatoes, peppers, squash and beans. Some tree leaves were showing signs of scorching, especially along their edges. Dogwoods and maples showed signs of water stress. Milk production was down because of the affect the heat had on the cows. Dating back to 1895, it was the ninth warmest and fourteenth driest September on record for the state of Pennsylvania. Across eastern Pennsylvania, monthly county precipitation averages ranged from 0.6 inches in Philadelphia County to 1.6 inches in Chester County. Normal is about 4.0 inches. The September average monthly temperature of 73.8 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport was the fourth warmest September on record and the 0.21 inches of precipitation was the second driest September on record. At the Lehigh Valley International Airport, the monthly mean temperature of 68.7 degrees was the fourth warmest September on record and the 0.76 inches of precipitation was the third driest September on record. In Reading (Berks County), the monthly average temperature of 70.5 degrees was 5.1 degrees warmer than normal and the monthly precipitation total of 0.56 inches was 3.80 inches drier than normal. In Mount Pocono (Monroe County), the monthly average temperature of 62.4 degrees was 5.0 degrees warmer than normal and the monthly precipitation total of 2.28 inches was 2.70 inches drier than normal.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of large tree limbs and wires in the northeastern part of Philadelphia. PECO Energy reported about 30,000 of its customers in southeastern Pennsylvania lost power including the city of Philadelphia.

A severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees and several tree limbs and wires throughout the Lehigh Valley. In Northampton County, the greatest reported wind damage was in Belfast (Plainfield Township). About 2,000 homes and businesses lost power in Northampton County.

A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in the Borough of Emmaus. Wires were dangling at only about 15 feet above Interstate 78 in the borough as tractor-trailers barely passed under them. About 1,000 homes and businesses lost power in Lehigh County.

A second severe thunderstorm knocked down more trees and wires in southeastern Berks County from Union Township northeast through Boyertown. About 6,800 homes and businesses (mainly around Boyertown) lost power from the pair of severe thunderstorms.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and several large tree limbs and wires across the southern tier of Montgomery County near the Philadelphia border. Most of the wind damage was reported in Upper Moreland and Lower Merion Townships. In the Bryn Mawr section of Lower Merion Township, a downed tree blocked a roadway. PECO Energy reported about 30,000 of its customers in southeastern Pennsylvania lost power including Montgomery County.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and power lines in Paradise and Price Townships.

Gusty northwest winds associated with showers along a cold frontal passage downed a few trees and power lines mainly in the lower (southeastern) part of Bucks County. PECO Energy reported that about 8,000 customers in their southeastern Pennsylvania service area lost power, the bulk (6,100 customers) were in Bucks County. All power was restored within two hours. The power outages did affect the High School within Warminster Township. One downed large tree blocked a roadway in Upper Southampton Township. Downed wires were also reported in Falls, Middletown and Lower Makefield Townships.
A severe thunderstorm tore down trees, tree limbs and wires across the northern part of Berks County. About 1,500 homes and businesses lost power in Hamburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed power poles.
Thunderstorm winds downed trees.
Thunderstorm winds downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another run of unseasonably hot weather affected eastern Pennsylvania from August 2nd through the 5th. High temperatures reached at least into the mid 90s each afternoon. The heat resulted in five more heat related deaths (4 in Philadelphia, 1 in Montgomery County). Hospitals saw an increase in cases of heat exhaustion and respiratory related illnesses. Even some of the Philadelphia Eagles were affected by the heat as they had to cut their practices short at Lehigh University. Seeking relief from the heat helped cause the drowning of a 17 year-old boy in Upper Providence Township (Montgomery County). He was swimming with his friends in the Perkiomen Creek in Lower Perkiomen Valley Park when he drowned. Once again the city of Philadelphia declared a code red as outreach teams helped the homeless by offering them cold water and air conditioned shelters to escape the heat. The PJM Interconnection Electricity Grid Operator asked for public conservation of electricity. The excessive heat did put a strain on electrical systems and caused widely scattered power outages in Berks County.A weak cold front moved through eastern Pennsylvania during the night of the 5th and brought temporary relief from the heat and humidity. Highest temperatures included 98 degrees in West Chester (Chester County) and Easton (Northampton County), 96 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, Reading (Berks County) and the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 95 degrees in Doylestown and Neshaminy Falls (Bucks County) and 93 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County).

A home was hit by lightning in the Penn Valley area. A total of about $85,000 in damage was done to the home, with about $50,000 in damage to the residents art collection. The couple in the home escaped the electrical surge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down several trees in Wharton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blown down.
Thunderstorm winds produced Hen Egg size hail /2.00 inch/ in Wellsboro.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees in Charleston Township east of Wellsboro.
A line of thunderstorms moved across Warren County, knocking down trees throughout the county.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires in Bradford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of several creeks within Philadelphia in the Roxborough and northeast portion of the city. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached three inches. A trained Skywarn Spotter measured 2.25 inches of rain within 45 minutes. Actual storm totals included 2.93 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 2.53 inches in Roxborough. The Frankford Creek at Castor Avenue was above its 7 foot flood stage from 728 p.m. EDT through 810 p.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 8.14 feet at 745 p.m. EDT on the 17th. The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 712 p.m. EDT on the 17th through 221 a.m. EDT on the 18th. It crested at 9.23 feet at 10 p.m. EDT on the 17th.
A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees and wires in southeastern Monroe County, particularly in and around Stroudsburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires in Westline.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees in Wilcox.
At Erie International Airport, a wind gust was measured at 58 mph.
Several trees were reported down.
Several trees and power lines were reported down.
Numerous trees and power lines were reported down across the county.

A tornado with estimated wind speeds of 85 to 95 mph touched down near the intersection of Route 16 and Tract Road near the Maryland border in far southern Adams County. The tornado moved northeast ending near Middle Creek. Several outbuilding and barn roofs were damaged, and numerous large trees were uprooted and snapped.  Superficial damage to several homes (siding, shingles, chimney bricks) also occurred.  A 7000 lb horse trailer was overturned and moved several feet near the intersection of Tract and Orchard Roads. There were no injuries or deaths.

An F1 tornado was confirmed in Adams County with top wind speeds estimated at 75 to 85 mph. The tornado began near the intersection of Route 30 and Crooked Creek Road in Franklin Township. It then moved north-northeast across Mummasburg then ended near the Conewago Creek before Zeigler Mill Road. Swirling patterns were noted  in corn fields, but the funnel damage path was quite narrow. Damage was mainly in the F0 range, except in Mummasburg where a dozen medium-sized trees were uprooted and snapped and a chain link fence bent out of shape, and winds were estimated as F1.  Superficial damage was noted  to several homes (siding, shingles, gutters) and a one-story church. There were no injuries or deaths.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires in Landisburg.
Several trees were reported down.
Penny size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and very humid air mass enveloped southeastern Pennsylvania on the 18th and 19th. High temperatures reached into the lower 90s, but dewpoint temperatures were in the mid 70s. The combination produced afternoon heat indices of between 100 and 105. In addition, the high dewpoints prevented temperatures from falling lower than the 70s at night. The low temperature at the Philadelphia International Airport the morning of the 19th was 77 degrees. The excessive heat caused the death of six elderly people within the city of Philadelphia. Most had non-working air conditioners, did not turn the air conditioner on or no air-conditioning at all. The excessive heat activated special summer heat programs in Philadelphia. Over 200 fans were distributed to the elderly. Homes of the elderly were visited by field teams, "The Heatline" was activated by the Philadelphia Corporation for the Elderly, there was an enhanced daytime outreach for the homeless and the buddy system to check on the elderly was activated.A cold front that moved through the state late in the day on the 19th, did not lower temperatures much, but humidity levels were much lower on the 20th through the 24th. Dew point temperatures on the afternoon of the 20th were 10 to 15 degrees lower than they were the two previous days. The highest temperature at the Philadelphia International Airport was 92 degrees.

A severe thunderstorm knocked over several large trees in Yardley Borough and adjacent Lower Makefield Township. Several homes were damaged by the downed trees in both municipalities. In Yardley a downed tree crushed the porch and damaged the roof of one home. Minor structural damage occurred to two other homes within the borough. In Lower Makefield Township, another downed large tree brutalized the porch and damaged the roof of another home. Two vehicles were briefly trapped on Taylorsville Road when trees fell down on both sides of them. Dolington Road remained closed through the night because of downed trees. About 8,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county.

A severe thunderstorm caused numerous lightning strikes across the county and some damaging wind gusts in the central part of the county. Several trees were knocked down at the Wyomissing Pools. About 11,100 homes and businesses lost power in 70 trouble spots throughout the county. The greatest concentration of outages was in downtown Reading. About 2,000 cloud-to-ground lightning bolts occurred during the early evening in and around Berks County. In Kenhorst Borough, a lightning strike blew out the transformers at a swim club. The facility remained closed for three days until they were replaced. Elsewhere in the county, all power was restored by the next morning (the 14th).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires along Irishtown Road 3 miles southwest of New Oxford.
Thunderstorm winds blew the roof off a shed along Storm Store Road in Mount Pleasant Township about 4 miles north-northeast of Bonneauville.
Thunderstorms produced nickel size /0.88 inch/ and quarter size /1.00 inch/ hail about 2 miles southwest of New Oxford. Numerous limbs and trees were also reported down.
Thunderstorms produced nickel size /0.88 inch/ to quarter size /1.00 inch/ hail in Abbottstown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and Flash Flooding in Adams County. Flooding was reported in the Bonneauville area, and in Littlestown. Roads were closed in Mount Pleasant Township along the 900 block of Stone Bridge Road. Centennial Road and Storm Store Road were also closed. Flooding was also reported in Littlestown where Barlow and Two Tavern Roads were closed, and in the town of Centennial.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees, wires and utility poles in the town of Central. Thunderstorms also produced quarter size /1.00 inch/ hail.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees and wires 9 miles north of Muncy Valley.

A stronger microburst struck Sugarcreek and West Franklin townships, near Cowansville, between 115 AM and 130 AM EDT on the 31st. Numerous trees were toppled and large branches snapped off. Several buildings had structural damage. At a construction company, 2 cinder block walls were blown down. Part of a roof blown off another building. Minor damage to several houses near the construction company. Several sheds were destroyed. A camper was overturned. Microburst was 300 yards wide and three quarters of a mile in length. Maximum wind estimated 90 mph (78 kts).  (KATRINA)
Numerous trees and power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most hottest and humid air mass of the summer so far occupied Eastern Pennsylvania from the 25th through the 27th. This air mass had as its origin the desert southwest in the United States and a ridge of high pressure aloft brought this air mass east with it through the rest of the country. The hottest and most humid day was the 27th. This was the hottest day of the year so far for most places and the highest temperatures seen in many areas since August of 2002. The combination of high temperatures and humidity levels pushed afternoon heat indices to between 105 and 110 degrees around the Greater Philadelphia Metropolitan Area on the 27th. Five elderly persons succumbed to the heat within Philadelphia and two elderly persons died because of the heat in Montgomery County. Most were found in homes with no working air conditioners or air conditioners at all. But, an 88-year-old woman in Pottstown (Montgomery County) was found dead slumped over the steering wheel of her vehicle in a shopping center. The car doors and windows were closed. Hospitals saw an increase in the number of patients complaining about dizziness, allergies and breathing difficulties. Schools in Philadelphia whether they had air conditioning or not dismissed the children early. Delaware Park cancelled their horse racing card on the 27th. Outdoor activities for prisoners in Philadelphia were cancelled. The PJM Interconnection recorded a world record for electricity usage on the 26th: 135,000 megawatts. The PJM coordinates the movement of electricity in Pennsylvania and twelve other states from North Carolina to Illinois. On the 27th, PJM lowered voltage to certain customers by five percent due to the stress on the high voltage lines. Locally PECO Energy (in and around Philadelphia) established back-to-back record usage days of 8,209 megawatts in an hour on the 26th and 8,329 megawatt in an hour on the 27th. Both surpassed the previous record of 8,164 megawatts set in August of 2002. The utility also established an all-time daily record usage of 165.7 million kilowatts surpassing the previous record of 163 million kilowatts set in July of 1999.  Other utilities also set new usage records including Metropolitan Edison (2,792 megawatts in an hour) and Pennsylvania Power and Light (7,035 megawatts in an hour). The excessive heat activated special summer heat programs in Philadelphia. Homes of the elderly were visited by field teams, "The Heatline" was activated by the Philadelphia Corporation for the Elderly, there was an enhanced daytime outreach for the homeless and the buddy system to check on the elderly was activated. Chester County distributed all the available fans they had to the elderly. Delaware County encouraged the elderly to visit their senior citizen centers. In the Lehigh Valley, free rides were given on air-conditioned buses by the Lehigh and Northampton Transportation Authority. A code red was declared by Montgomery County. People were not the only ones suffering as repair shops were swamped with air-conditioning repairs for vehicles. Farmers were also noting that livestock was also affected by the heat as they were not eating much and were not gaining weight and/or producing less milk.A cold front abruptly ended the hot weather as it moved through the eastern part of the state late in the afternoon and early in the evening on the 27th.  The highest temperatures included 98 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and West Chester (Chester County), 96 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County), 95 degrees in Reading (Berks County), 94 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and Pottstown (Montgomery County) and 93 degrees in Forks Township (Northampton County).

This air mass had as its origin the desert southwest in the United States and a ridge of high pressure aloft brought this air mass east with it through the rest of the country. The hottest and most humid day was the 27th. This was the hottest day of the year so far for most places and the highest temperatures seen in many areas since August of 2002. The combination of high temperatures and humidity levels pushed afternoon heat indices to between 105 and 110 degrees around the Greater Philadelphia Metropolitan Area on the 27th. Five elderly persons succumbed to the heat within Philadelphia and two elderly persons died because of the heat in Montgomery County. Most were found in homes with no working air conditioners or air conditioners at all. But, an 88-year-old woman in Pottstown (Montgomery County) was found dead slumped over the steering wheel of her vehicle in a shopping center. The car doors and windows were closed. Hospitals saw an increase in the number of patients complaining about dizziness, allergies and breathing difficulties. Schools in Philadelphia whether they had air conditioning or not dismissed the children early. Delaware Park cancelled their horse racing card on the 27th. Outdoor activities for prisoners in Philadelphia were cancelled. The PJM Interconnection recorded a world record for electricity usage on the 26th: 135,000 megawatts. The PJM coordinates the movement of electricity in Pennsylvania and twelve other states from North Carolina to Illinois. On the 27th, PJM lowered voltage to certain customers by five percent due to the stress on the high voltage lines. Locally PECO Energy (in and around Philadelphia) established back-to-back record usage days of 8,209 megawatts in an hour on the 26th and 8,329 megawatt in an hour on the 27th. Both surpassed the previous record of 8,164 megawatts set in August of 2002. The utility also established an all-time daily record usage of 165.7 million kilowatts surpassing the previous record of 163 million kilowatts set in July of 1999.  Other utilities also set new usage records including Metropolitan Edison (2,792 megawatts in an hour) and Pennsylvania Power and Light (7,035 megawatts in an hour). The excessive heat activated special summer heat programs in Philadelphia. Homes of the elderly were visited by field teams, "The Heatline" was activated by the Philadelphia Corporation for the Elderly, there was an enhanced daytime outreach for the homeless and the buddy system to check on the elderly was activated. Chester County distributed all the available fans they had to the elderly. Delaware County encouraged the elderly to visit their senior citizen centers. In the Lehigh Valley, free rides were given on air-conditioned buses by the Lehigh and Northampton Transportation Authority. A code red was declared by Montgomery County. People were not the only ones suffering as repair shops were swamped with air-conditioning repairs for vehicles. Farmers were also noting that livestock was also affected by the heat as they were not eating much and were not gaining weight and/or producing less milk.A cold front abruptly ended the hot weather as it moved through the eastern part of the state late in the afternoon and early in the evening on the 27th.  The highest temperatures included 98 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and West Chester (Chester County), 96 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County), 95 degrees in Reading (Berks County), 94 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and Pottstown (Montgomery County) and 93 degrees in Forks Township (Northampton County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong F1 tornado with winds up to 110 mph touched down between Dillsburg in York County and Mechanicsburg in Cumberland County.  Specifically, the tornado began about 12:17 am EST near the intersection of Old Route 15 and Creek Road just south of the Rose Garden subdivision in York County.  The tornado moved north across Yellow Breeches Creek into Cumberland County around 12:19 am EST through the Ashcombe Farms South Development.  It ended along Stumpstown Road about a half mile west of Route 15 at 12:22 am EST.  The tornado path was about two and one half miles in length, and 100 yards wide.
Trees and power lines blown down onto a house.
Between Route 19 (Washington Rd) and Mc Clelland Rd, just west of Borland Manor, there were 18 instances  of trees and power lines blown down. A few trees fell on cars and against houses.
Roads closed by flood.
Trees and power lines down.
Large tree limbs blown down.

As Katrina moved north of the area (through northeast Ohio), a microburst hit the northern part of Sarver between 12 AM and 1215 AM EDT on the 31st. Numerous trees were toppled or snapped off. Microburst was 300 yards wide and sporadically half a mile in length. Maximum wind estimated 65 mph (57 kts). (KATRINA)
Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees in Montgomery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The gust front associated with a line of severe thunderstorms produced wind gusts up to 63 mph within Philadelphia and downed about 100 trees throughout the city particularly in the northwest and northeast part of the city. The downed trees caused power outages. A Skywarn spotter measured a wind gust of 63 mph in Roxborough.  About 9,000 homes and businesses lost power in Philadelphia. Power was not fully restored until the 28th.

The gust front from a line of severe thunderstorms knocked down trees and wires in Bristol Township. About 7,000 homes and businesses lost power in Bucks County. Power was not fully restored until the 28th.

A persistent group of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flooding of streams and poor drainage flooding in the northwestern part of Berks County. Doppler Radar storm totals reached around three inches.
A severe thunderstorm dropped penny to nickel size hail in Tobyhanna Township.

A severe thunderstorm caused numerous lightning strikes across the county and some damaging wind gusts in the central part of the county. Several trees were knocked down at the Wyomissing Pools. About 11,100 homes and businesses lost power in 70 trouble spots throughout the county. The greatest concentration of outages was in downtown Reading. About 2,000 cloud-to-ground lightning bolts occurred during the early evening in and around Berks County. In Kenhorst Borough, a lightning strike blew out the transformers at a swim club. The facility remained closed for three days until they were replaced. Elsewhere in the county, all power was restored by the next morning (the 14th).

It ended along Stumpstown Road about a half mile west of Route 15 at 12:22 am EST.  The tornado path was about two and one half miles in length, and 100 yards wide.
Penny size hail was observed.
Penny size hail was observed.

Thunderstorm winds caused damage in the York Haven area. Damage began about 5 miles Northwest of York Haven. Trees were down in Newberry Township along the 3200 and 3400 Block of  York Haven Road. Trees were also downed between York Haven Road and Pear Avenue. Damage extended to about 5 miles southeast of York Haven, where trees were also reported down in East Manchester Township along the 100 block of Manchester Street, North George Street, and Wago Road. Additional trees and wires were noted down in the town of Manchester, along Mill Creek Road.

A strong F1 tornado with winds up to 100 mph touched down just northeast of Gettysburg and tracked northeastward to near Heidlersburg in Adams County. Specifically, the tornado touched down about 1138 pm EST near the intersection of Hunterstown Road and Shealer Road about one half mile west of the intersection of Routes 15 and 30. The tornado touched down intermittently at locations along a seven mile path just to the east of Route 15, ending near the intersection of Oxford Road and Route 234 near Heidlersburg at 1150 pm EST.  The tornado path damage width varied from 100 to 300 yards along its path.The tornado destroyed several barns and sheds and removed some outbuilding roofs. Additionally, there was tree damage and mostly superficial damage to several homes in its path (siding and shingles). Trees were uprooted and snapped along the path as well. There were no injuries or deaths.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees on Route 125 south of Burnside.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in the southwest and northeast part of the city. The downed trees damaged a couple of vehicles and homes. Penny size hail also fell during the severe thunderstorm.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in the southwest and northeast part of the city. The downed trees damaged a couple of vehicles and homes. Penny size hail also fell during the severe thunderstorm.
A thunderstorm cluster with frequent lightning caused about 3,000 Pennsylvania Power and Light customers to lose power during the night of July 1st.
Lightning strikes caused about 300 homes and businesses to lose power in Leesport for about two hours during the evening of the 5th.

Two persons were struck by lightning within the Hickory Run State Park at Camp Site C in Kidder Township. They were both seriously injured. The woman was struck by lightning as she placed her hand on the car door. A male employee of the park was struck as he exited a manhole.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey team composed of National Weather Service employees along with Lycoming County and Local Emergency Management Personnel confirmed that an F1 tornado touched down briefly in Lycoming County near the town of Barbours very early on the morning of August 31.The tornado touched down just southeast of the town of Barbours, along Route 87 about one half mile south of the intersection of Route 87 and Dunwoody Road. The tornado traveled northeast and lifted on Dunwoody Road near the Barbours Municipal Building.The path length of the tornado was about three quarters of a mile, and the path width was about 100 yards. Wind speeds were estimated around 90 mph, putting it in the middle of the F1 range. Damage was confined mainly to trees, as the tornado traveled through a heavily wooded area. There were no injuries or deaths associated with the tornado.The tornado damage was embedded in a larger swath of sporadic straight line wind damage, that extended about 2 miles from the northern edge of the Tiadaghton State Forest to the edge of the State Game Lands northeast of the town of Barbours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy caused flooding along the East Branch of the Brandywine Creek. Rain began around midnight EDT on the 8th and fell heavy at times around sunrise and again around Noon EDT. Storm totals averaged between 2 and 3 inches. This was enough heavy rain to push the East Branch of the Brandywine Creek over its banks. In Downingtown, the creek was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1247 p.m. EDT through 355 p.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 7.63 feet at 215 p.m. EDT. The heavy rain combined with east winds of 20 to 30 mph to push over several weak trees and tree limbs throughout the county and southeastern Pennsylvania. About 2,500 homes and businesses lost power throughout southeastern Pennsylvania. In Chester County, U.S. Route 322 was closed for most of the daylight hours in East Brandywine Township because of a downed tree and wires from 11 a.m. EDT onward. Specific storm totals included 2.96 inches in Downingtown, 2.85 inches in Modena and 2.48 inches in Honey Brook. The remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy moved from near Atlanta, Georgia at 8 a.m. EDT on the 7th northeast to near Washington, D.C. at 8 a.m. EDT on the 8th, lower Delaware at 2 p.m. EDT on the 8th and about 100 miles east of Atlantic City at 8 p.m. EDT on the 8th.

A line of severe thunderstorms knocked down several trees and wires throughout Montgomery County. The greatest concentration of reported wind damage were in and around Jenkintown and Royersford. About 16,000 homes and businesses lost power in Montgomery County. Power was not fully restored until the 28th.
A thunderstorm cluster with frequent lightning caused about 3,000 Pennsylvania Power and Light customers to lose power during the night of July 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The northwest flow around an intensifying northeaster brought strong winds to eastern Pennsylvania during the late morning and early afternoon on the 22nd. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 46 mph in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 44 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 41 mph in Reading (Berks County), 40 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 38 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport. The low pressure system responsible for the strong winds intensified from a 1007mb low near Jacksonville, Florida at 7 a.m. EST on the 21st, to a 996mb low near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 21st, to a 979mb low about 100 miles east of Atlantic City, New Jersey at 7 a.m. EST on the 22nd, to a 972mb low near Boston, Massachusetts at 1 p.m. EST on the 22nd to a 968mb low in northeastern Maine at 7p.m. EST on the 22nd. The strongest winds occurred as the intensifying low pressure system moved through southern New England.

Gusty northwest winds buffeted eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the first half of the day on the 10th. The northwest winds were caused by the difference in surface pressure between an intensifying low pressure system moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley in Canada and a high pressure system building east from the central Mississippi Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 43 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and 41 mph in Doylestown (Bucks County).

A slow moving cold front coupled with remnant moisture from Tropical Storm Tammy produced periods of heavy rain across the Poconos from the afternoon of the 7th until 300 a.m. EDT on the 8th and again during the late morning into the late afternoon on the 8th. Storm totals averaged between 3 and 8 inches and caused poor drainage flooding. But the dry weather over the previous seven weeks helped minimize the stream and river flooding in the county. The only gaged waterway to reach flood stage was the Aquashicola Creek in Palmerton. The creek was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1107 p.m. EDT on the 8th through 311 a.m. EDT on the 9th. It crested at 7.34 feet at 130 a.m. EDT on the 9th. Storm totals included 8.00 inches in Albrightsville, 7.47 inches in Lehighton, 7.04 inches in Twamensing, 5.24 inches in Packer Township, 3.56 inches at the F.E. Walter Dam and 3.07 inches at the Beltzville Lake. This heavy rain helped the state of Pennsylvania have its second wettest October on record dating back to 1895. The statewide average precipitation total was 6.49 inches, 3.31 inches wetter than normal. Only 1976 (6.59 inches) was wetter. At the Pocono Regional Airport in Monroe County, the monthly precipitation total of 12.80 inches was 9.04 inches wetter than normal. The cold front moved from near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania at 8 p.m. EDT on the 7th, to the Susquehanna Valley in Pennsylvania at 2 a.m. EDT on the 8th, to the Poconos and Berks County in Pennsylvania and just west of Chesapeake Bay in Maryland at 8 a.m. EDT on the 8th. The cold front stalled as waves of low pressure road northeast along it. At 2 p.m. EDT on the 8th, the cold front just edged into northwestern New Jersey and the Philadelphia northwest suburbs and was still west of Chesapeake Bay in Maryland. At 8 p.m. EDT that evening the cold front barely moved into the Philadelphia New Jersey suburbs and the Delmarva Peninsula. The cold front and waves of low pressure cleared the Delaware and New Jersey coasts between Midnight EDT and 2 a.m. EDT on the 9th. The slow movement and stalling coupled with an unstable air mass and tropical moisture associated with Tammy helped enhance the torrential downpours. The flooding would have been even worse if not for the unseasonably dry weather that preceded this event from the middle of August.

A slow moving cold front coupled with remnant moisture from Tropical Storm Tammy produced periods of heavy rain across the Poconos from the afternoon of the 7th until 300 a.m. EDT on the 8th and again during the late morning into the late afternoon on the 8th. Storm totals averaged between 5 and 10 inches and caused poor drainage flooding. But the dry weather over the previous seven weeks helped reduce the stream and river flooding in the county. The Delaware River did not flood. In Smithfield Township, part of Shawnee Valley Housing Development was evacuated when a mudslide occurred. Baseball fields along the Brodhead Creek flooded. Flooding and mudslides closed Pennsylvania State Route 191 and 447 in Stroud Township. Roadway shoulders were washed out on Pennsylvania State Routes 611, 447, 940, 191 and U.S. Route 209. The only gaged waterway to reach flood stage was the Brodhead Creek in Minisink Hills. The creek was above its 10 foot flood stage from 511 p.m. through 915 p.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 10.55 feet at 715 p.m. EDT. Storm totals included 11.00 inches in Saylorsburg, 10.83 inches in Bossardville, 9.44 inches in East Stroudsburg, 9.16 inches in Brodheadsville, 9.13 inches in Tannersville, 6.73 inches in Pocono Summit, 5.54 inches in Blakeslee and 4.63 inches in Stoddartsville. The 9.44 inch event total in East Stroudsburg was the greatest one storm total on record even surpassing the storm totals from Hurricanes Connie and Diane in 1955, Floyd in 1999 and Ivan in 2004. This heavy rain helped the state of Pennsylvania have its second wettest October on record dating back to 1895. The statewide average precipitation total was 6.49 inches, 3.31 inches wetter than normal. Only 1976 (6.59 inches) was wetter. At the Pocono Regional Airport in Mount Pocono, the monthly precipitation total of 12.80 inches was 9.04 inches wetter than normal. The northwest flow around an intensifying northeaster brought strong winds to eastern Pennsylvania during the late morning and early afternoon on the 22nd. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 46 mph in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 44 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 41 mph in Reading (Berks County), 40 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 38 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport. The low pressure system responsible for the strong winds intensified from a 1007mb low near Jacksonville, Florida at 7 a.m. EST on the 21st, to a 996mb low near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 21st, to a 979mb low about 100 miles east of Atlantic City, New Jersey at 7 a.m. EST on the 22nd, to a 972mb low near Boston, Massachusetts at 1 p.m. EST on the 22nd to a 968mb low in northeastern Maine at 7p.m. EST on the 22nd. The strongest winds occurred as the intensifying low pressure system moved through southern New England.

The combination of being near the center of a high pressure system that helped produce light winds and a deep snowpack caused record or near record breaking low temperatures on the morning of the 14th from the Lehigh Valley northward. For many this was the coldest day of the meteorological winter season. The low of 4 degrees below zero in East Stroudsburg tied the record for the day. The low of 2 degrees below zero at the Lehigh Valley International Airport established a new record low for the day. Six people in Allentown (Lehigh County) were sickened by carbon monoxide emissions from their faulty heating unit. The cold weather caused an increase in demand for electricity and Pennsylvania Power and Light came within 100 megawatts (7,174) between 7 and 8 a.m. EST of tying their winter hourly megawatt usage record set in December 20, 2004.

A persistent onshore flow for nearly a week caused higher than normal tides and this culminated with minor tidal flooding during the evening high tide on the 13th along the Delaware River. At pier 12 in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, the high tide reached 8.67 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding begins at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.The old frontal boundary that produced the heavy rain on the 8th stalled in the nearby Atlantic. A series of low pressure systems lifted north along this boundary. A high pressure system over nearby Canada and New England helped block any northward or eastward progression of the frontal boundary and the low pressure systems that rode northward along the front until the 14th. The strongest onshore flow between the high pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes and one of the low pressure systems east of the Delmarva Peninsula occurred on the 13th. The persistent onshore flow (of varying strength) lasted for about a week until a cold frontal passage moved through the region during the late afternoon on the 15th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused Penns Creek at Penns Creek to flood. The creek exceeded flood stage of 8 feet at 22:45 EST on the 29th, crested at 8.24 feet at 00:15 EST on the 30th, then fell back below flood stage at 06:30 EST on the 30th.

Heavy rain caused Sherman Creek at Shermans Dale to flood. The creek exceeded flood stage of 9 feet at 04:00 EST on the 30th, crested at 9.37 feet at 05:30 EST on the 30th, then fell back below flood stage at 07:45 EST on the 30th.

Heavy rain caused Aughwick Creek at Shirleysburg to flood. The creek exceeded flood stage of 10 feet at 00:30 EST on the 30th, crested at 12.15 feet at 12:15 EST on the 30th, then fell back below flood stage at 16:30 EST on the 30th.

Heavy rain caused the West Branch Susquehanna River at Montgomery to flood. The river exceeded flood stage of 17 feet at 09:00 EST on the 30th, crested at 19.7 feet at 22:00 EST on the 30th, then fell back below flood stage at 01:00 EST on the 2nd of December.

The combination of heavy rain and strong southerly winds helped knock down trees, tree limbs and wires in the Lehigh Valley during the late afternoon and early evening of the 29th. About 2,500 homes and businesses lost power, most of them within Allentown and Macungie in Lehigh County.  Most of the outages were restored by Midnight EST that night. The greatest impact occurred along U.S. Route 22 in Lehigh County. All of the eastbound lanes and one westbound lane were closed for about 90 minutes from 615 p.m. through 745 p.m. EST because of downed poles and wires. The power outages affected stores near U.S. Route 22 in Whitehall Township (Lehigh County). The peak wind gust at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was 41 mph.

Lake Effect snow bands developed during the morning hours of Wednesday December 7th, producing highly localized snowfall amounts of 6 to 8 inches across northwest portions of Warren County.  The snow bands diminished during the afternoon hours.

A low pressure system developed along the Gulf Coast on Thursday December 8th, and then tracked northeast toward the mid Atlantic Coast on Friday, December 9th.  This produced a swath of moderate to locally heavy snowfall across central Pennsylvania from late Thursday night into early Friday morning. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches, with the highest amounts across the lower and middle Susquehanna Valley region. This storm had a particularly negative impact on the morning commute on Friday across central Pennsylvania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of snow melt and the precipitation that fell from the afternoon of the15th into the early morning of the 16th caused minor flooding along a couple of creeks in Montgomery County. The Wissahickon Creek at Fort Washington was above its 9 foot flood stage from 521 a.m. EST through 1037 a.m. EST on the 16th. It crested at 10.69 feet at 8 a.m. EST. The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Schwenksville was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1136 a.m. EST through 1230 p.m. EST on the 16th. It crested at 7.04 feet at Noon EST. Precipitation totals included 1.63 inches in Hatboro, 1.62 at Willow Grove, 1.28 inches in Pottstown, 1.26 inches at Green Lane and 1.20 inches in Palm. The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed on the Gulf Coast early on the 15th. A broad southerly flow brought moisture into the region. The low itself moved northeast through the Delaware Valley overnight on the 15th and was located near New York City at sunrise on the 16th. The track helped bring moisture from both the Atlantic Ocean and the Gulf of Mexico and dropped it across Montgomery County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very cold air blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the early morning hours of the 19th.  The heaviest snow fell just before daybreak and also around midday.  6 to 10 inches of snow was reported across much of inland Erie County.   A peak total of 11.7 inches was measured at Franklin Center.  The snow was dry and fluffy and westerly winds gusting to 25 mph caused much blowing and drifting snow.   Many accidents occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front associated with a low pressure system from the Gulf of Mexico caused a protracted precipitation event to occur in Pennsylvania. This was complicated by a very cold air mass that was in place over the state at the start. Some record breaking low temperatures occurred just the morning before. Snow overspread the region between 1 p.m. EST and 3 p.m. EST. The warm air had an easier time advancing aloft than near the ground as an unseasonably cold air mass was still in place. The snow changed to freezing rain about an hour after precipitation started around Philadelphia (4 p.m. EST). It took a bit longer elsewhere changing to freezing rain around 6 p.m. EST in the Philadelphia northwestern suburbs and Berks County, 7 p.m. EST in the Lehigh Valley and 8 p.m. EST in the Poconos north. Snowfall accumulations were an inch or less, except in the Poconos where up to three inches accumulated. Warmer air from the relatively warm Atlantic Ocean changed the freezing rain to plain rain around Philadelphia between 6 p.m. and 7 p.m. EST, but took until around 11 p.m. EST for the far Philadelphia northern suburbs and Berks County, around Midnight EST on the 16th in the Lehigh Valley and as late as 4 a.m. EST on the 16th in the Poconos. Before the change to plain rain occurred, around one quarter of an inch of ice accrued on exposed surfaces from the Poconos south into the northern Philadelphia suburbs.Travel on untreated roadways were treacherous. In Berks County, about 30 crashes were reported. Westbound Interstate 78 was closed in Greenwich Township because a tractor-trailer rolled onto its side. Sections of U.S. Route 422 were closed in Amity Township in both directions because of accidents. In the Lehigh Valley numerous accidents occurred as vehicles slid off roads, rolled over or skidded into each other. Other vehicles became stuck on the ice. Westbound Interstate 78 was closed near Hellertown (Northampton County). In Lower Saucon Township (Northampton County), four people were injured in a crash that blocked another section of Interstate 78 for three hours. A tractor-trailer was struck by two vehicles. Pennsylvania State Routes 191 and 512 were ice covered and vehicles were sliding off of Pennsylvania State Route 33. Carbon County in the Poconos also reported numerous crashes and many vehicles that slid off roads. Many schools were closed on the 16th in the Poconos and the Lehigh Valley. Even the Philadelphia Flyers were not immune from the effects of the ice as two separate multi-car crashes on the Walt Whitman Bridge closed the span for one hour. Two-thirds of the team was stuck in the traffic tie-up and the start of the game was delayed.Snowfall accumulations included 3.0 inches in Pocono Summit (Monore County), 2.6 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County) and 0.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed on the Gulf Coast early on the 15th. A broad southerly brought moisture and much milder air into the region. The low itself moved northeast through the Delaware valley overnight on the 5th and was located near New York City at sunrise on the 16th. The track helped bring relatively warm air from the nearby Atlantic Ocean into the region and helped change the freezing rain to plain rain.

A low pressure system that developed along the Gulf Coast States on the 5th moved quickly northeast and dropped heavy snow across the local Philadelphia area with lighter snow in the northwestern Philadelphia suburbs. Little if any snow fell from Berks County and the Lehigh Valley northward. The snow began during the late afternoon and early evening of the 5th, but fell lightly through the evening. The heaviest snow fell after Midnight EST on the 6th. The snow ended around sunrise on the 6th. Accumulations averaged two to four inches with the highest amounts in the local Philadelphia area. Specific accumulations included 4.0 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and Havertown (Delaware County), 3.5 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County) and Neshaminy Falls (Bucks County), 2.8 inches in Unionville (Chester County), 2.5 inches in New Hope (Bucks County), 1.5 inches in Valley Forge (Chester County), 1.2 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County) and 0.5 inches in Royersford (Montgomery County), Fricks (Bucks County) and Glenmoore (Chester County)  A low pressure system developed over Georgia early on the 5th and moved northeast. The low deepened as it began to move off the coast of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina on Monday night the 5th. The low pressure system moved relatively quickly and was east of New England by noon on the 6th. The fast movement and the relative offshore track prevented heavy snow from getting much farther west than the local Philadelphia area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake Effect snow bands developed during the morning hours of Friday December 2nd over Warren County, and then expanded east during the afternoon and evening hours into McKean and Potter Counties. Snowfall rates of up to 2 inches per hour, combined with gusty winds to produce localized near-blizzard conditions during the late afternoon and evening hours. The snow bands gradually diminished in areal coverage and intensity during the early morning hours of Saturday December 3rd.  Localized snowfall amounts of up to 11 inches occurred in Potter County, while 12 to 16 inches occurred in northern Warren and McKean counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved east across western Pennsylvania on the morning of November 16th.   Eventually enough cold air filtered into the region behind this front to cause lake effect snow showers to develop over extreme Northwest Pennsylvania.  This occurred just before daybreak on the 17th as gusty westerly winds blew across Lake Erie.  The strongest bands of snow affected roughly the northern half of Erie County.  The heaviest snow fell during the late morning hours with visibilities below one half mile for a couple hours.   Westerly winds gusting to 30 mph accompanied the snow and caused considerable blowing and drifting snow.  Locations along and north of Interstate 90 saw accumulations of 6 to 8 inches with lesser amounts further south.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees.
Thunderstorm winds downed trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipped passed to the north of Lake Erie during the evening hours of November 23rd.  An arctic cold front trailing this low swept east across Northwest Pennsylvania early on the 24th.  Cold northwest winds behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to develop just before daybreak on the 24th.  These bands quickly intensified and by mid-morning, visibilities in some areas were less than one quarter mile.   Northwest winds gusting in excess of 30 mph accompanied the snow and caused considerable blowing and drifting.  The snow showers tappered to flurries during the early evening hours.  Snowfall totals of 8 to 16 inches were reported in both Crawford and inland Erie Counties by sunset on the 24th.  Then, after midnight on the 25th, an upper level disturbance rotated through the region.  This caused a new round of lake effect snow showers to develop.  This activity diminished during the afternoon of the 25th after another 6 to 9 inches of snow had fallen.  Two day totals for this event were 12 to 18 inches in many locations.  A peak of 23.5 inches was measured at Corry (Erie County) with 19 inches at Meadville  (Crawford County).

Gusty northwest winds buffeted eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the first half of the day on the 10th. The northwest winds were caused by the difference in surface pressure between an intensifying low pressure system moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley in Canada and a high pressure system building east from the central Mississippi Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 43 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and 41 mph in Doylestown (Bucks County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the West Branch Susquehanna River at Montgomery to flood. The river exceeded flood stage of 17 feet at 09:00 EST on the 30th, crested at 19.7 feet at 22:00 EST on the 30th, then fell back below flood stage at 01:00 EST on the 2nd of December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and an associated cold front moved east across the region on December 1st.  Cold air filtered into Northwest Pennsylvania behind this front and caused lake effect snow showers to develop late in the afternoon of the 1st.  The snow remained light through the evening hours with accumulations generally less than an inch through midnight.  The snow intensfied during the morning hours of the 2nd.  Visibilities at times were less than one half mile with snowfall rates in excess of an inch per.  The snow tapered to flurries during the late evening hours of the 2nd and ended altogether early on the 3rd.   Snowfall totals across Northwest Pennsylvania were generally 8 to 16 inches inland from Lake Erie.  Accumulations near the shoreline were generally 4 to 8 inches.  A peak total of nearly 21 inches was reported at Corry (Erie County) with 20 inches reported near Meadville in Crawford County.

Some light snow fell during the early morning of the 24th (Thanksgiving Day). Accumulations were generally less than one inch (Perkasie reported 0.7 inches). Air temperatures though were below freezing and this permitted the snow to stick to roadways, especially in the northern/upper part of Bucks County. The slick roads contributed to a traffic accident in which two men in a garbage truck slid off of westbound Pennsylvania State Route 313 into Lake Nockamixon in East Rockhill Township. They stood on the cab of the sinking truck until they were rescued by boat.  They were hospitalized for hypothermia. The bridge on Pennsylvania State Route 313 was damaged by the accident.

A low pressure system developed along the Gulf Coast early on Thursday December 15th, and then tracked northeast toward the mid Atlantic Coast from Thursday night into early Friday morning on December 16th.  This storm spread a swath of wintry precipitation across central Pennsylvania from early Thursday afternoon, into Thursday night and early Friday morning.  Precipitation began as snow, but then mixed with sleet and freezing rain by late Thursday afternoon. The mix of sleet and freezing rain then persisted through much of Thursday night, before changing back to light snow as it ended early Friday.Snowfall accumulations ranged from 1 to 3 inches across the lower Susquehanna Valley, to 3 to 6 inches across the central mountains, with 6 to 8 inches across the north central mountains. However, ice accretion of one quarter inch or more occurred across a large portion of the central mountains, and portions of the lower and mid Susquehanna Valley. The combination of snow and ice significantly impacted travel across much of central Pennsylvania Thursday evening into Friday morning. Several road closures occurred to due multi-vehicle accidents, including portions of Interstate 80 in Union County.

The northwest flow around an intensifying northeaster brought strong winds to eastern Pennsylvania during the late morning and early afternoon on the 22nd. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 46 mph in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 44 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 41 mph in Reading (Berks County), 40 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 38 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport. The low pressure system responsible for the strong winds intensified from a 1007mb low near Jacksonville, Florida at 7 a.m. EST on the 21st, to a 996mb low near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 21st, to a 979mb low about 100 miles east of Atlantic City, New Jersey at 7 a.m. EST on the 22nd, to a 972mb low near Boston, Massachusetts at 1 p.m. EST on the 22nd to a 968mb low in northeastern Maine at 7p.m. EST on the 22nd. The strongest winds occurred as the intensifying low pressure system moved through southern New England.

The combination of heavy rain and strong southerly winds helped knock down trees, tree limbs and wires in the Lehigh Valley during the late afternoon and early evening of the 29th. About 2,500 homes and businesses lost power, most of them within Allentown and Macungie in Lehigh County.  Most of the outages were restored by Midnight EST that night. The greatest impact occurred along U.S. Route 22 in Lehigh County. All of the eastbound lanes and one westbound lane were closed for about 90 minutes from 615 p.m. through 745 p.m. EST because of downed poles and wires. The power outages affected stores near U.S. Route 22 in Whitehall Township (Lehigh County). The peak wind gust at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was 41 mph.

Moisture that circulated around the intense low pressure system that brought rain to the region on Christmas Day helped produce a mixture of light snow, sleet and freezing rain to the Pocono Plateau the evening and overnight on the 26th. Precipitation amounts were mostly traces, but untreated roads and walks became slippery.

A very quick moving winter storm affected Eastern Pennsylvania during the first half of the day on December 9th. Snow began falling between 1 a.m. and 3 a.m. EST and fell heavy at times between 4 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST. In and close to Philadelphia, freezing rain mixed in with the snow from 7 a.m. EST until the precipitation ended around 10 a.m. EST. Precipitation went back to snow during heavier precipitation bursts. All precipitation ended across Eastern Pennsylvania between 9 a.m. and 11 a.m. EST. Snowfall accumulations ranged from around three inches in the local Philadelphia area to around 12 inches in the Poconos. Ice accretions around Philadelphia averaged around one-quarter of an inch.  Many school districts either never opened for the day or had delayed openings. There were numerous fender benders throughout the eastern part of the state as the heaviest snow could not have come at a more inopportune time, just before and during the morning commute time. Commutes averaged twice as long. About 10 percent of the flights scheduled from Philadelphia International Airport were cancelled. There was one reported weather related fatality. A 63-year-old woman was killed when she lost control of her vehicle near Den Road in Franklin Township (Chester County) and slid into the northbound lane of Pennsylvania State Route 896. The roadway was closed for three hours. In neighboring Montgomery County, 157 accidents were reported. This included a truck that slid into a commuter train in Lower Moreland Township. The SEPTA R3 line was shut down for one hour. In Berks County, numerous spin-outs and accidents were reported, even after the roads were cleared. Sections of Pennsylvania State Routes 61 and 183 and southbound U.S. Route 222 near Kutztown were closed because of accidents. The most serious reported accidents were at the intersection of Pennsylvania State Routes 12 and 73 in Ruscombmanor Township and on Pennsylvania State Route 100 in Washington Township. In Northampton County, in Easton a vehicle nearly skidded in to the Lehigh River. The snow also hampered firefighting efforts. In Salford Township (Montgomery County), nine people were homeless after their house on Hausman Road burned to the ground. The combination of the heavy snow, the house sitting on top of a hill at the end of a narrow road made access to the blaze difficult for firefighters. The combination of the snow and cold weather made many county health officials extend their "code blue" weather alerts. Specific accumulations included in Berks County 9.5 inches in Stony Run, 8.5 inches in Fleetwood and 8.0 inches in Reading; in Bucks County 9.5 inches in Springtown, 8.0 inches in New Hope and 6.5 inches in Perkasie; in Carbon County 12.0 inches in Albrightsville, 11.6 inches in Lehighton and 11.0 inches in Weatherly; in Chester County 9.0 inches in Coatesville and Downingtown, 7.5 inches in Glenmoore and 6.0 inches in West Chester; in Delaware County 3.8 inches in Chadds Ford and 3.5 inches in Havertown; in Lehigh County 10.5 inches in Alburtis, 9.1 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 8.3 inches within Allentown, 8.0 inches in Whitehall Township and 7.8 inches in Macungie; in Monroe County 14.0 inches in Pocono Summit, 12.0 inches in East Stroudsburg and 11.0 inches in Saylorsburg; in Montgomery County 7.8 inches in Trappe, 7.0 inches in Palm, 5.8 inches in King of Prussia and 4.0 inches in Wynnewood; in Northampton County 10.0 inches in Forks Township, 9.0 inches in Easton and 8.3 inches in Bethlehem and in Philadelphia 3.5 inches in Roxborough, 2.9 inches at Holme Circle and 2.2 inches at Philadelphia International Airport.   The winter storm was caused by the combination of a vigorous upper low from the Midwest and a Gulf of Mexico connection of moisture and low pressure systems. Two low pressure systems formed, one in response to the upper system in the Ohio Valley and one in the northeast Gulf of Mexico on Thursday the 8th. On Friday morning the 9th, the Ohio Valley low pressure system moved into the Great Lakes and the Gulf of Mexico low pressure system moved northeast to the North Carolina coast. A third low pressure system formed in response to the strong upper level support on the Delmarva Peninsula. The low pressure system along the Delmarva Peninsula became the primary low as both the Great Lakes and North Carolina Coast lows weakened. The Delmarva low pressure system rapidly intensified during the day on Friday the 9th. It went from 1009 millibars just west of the Long Island Buoy at 10 a.m. EST on the 9th, to a 996 mb low just south of Rhode Island at 1 p.m. EST on the 9th to about a 988 mb low between Masachusetts and Nova Scotia at 7 p.m. EST. At 7 a.m. EST on Saturday the 10th, the low was at least 979 mb just south of Newfoundland. In 24 hours the low deepened by about 36 millibars. In spite of its quick movement and the late intensification, heavy snow still fell across most of Eastern Pennsylvania.

This activity tapered to flurries around daybreak on the 7th.  The heaviest snow fell just inland of Lake Erie along the Interstate 90 corridor in northern Erie County.  6 to 10 inches of snow was reported in this area. Strong gusty west winds tore down one large tree in Lehighton. The tree fell on and took down wires on Bridge Street. This knocked out power for half of the town for about two hours. It also caused a massive traffic jam as Bridge Street was closed as repairs were made. The peak wind gust at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was 41 mph.

The first wintry event of the season spread a mixture of snow and freezing rain across Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 4th. Accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches with some higher amounts in the Poconos. Except for parts of the Poconos, the snow changed to freezing rain before ending during the middle of the morning with ice accretions of one to two tenths in most areas. While this event occurred on a Sunday morning and cut down on traffic, because it was the first widespread wintry precipitation event of the season, numerous accidents, some with serious injuries occurred. Snow began falling shortly after Midnight EST on the 1st. The snow changed to freezing rain from around 5 a.m. EST in the Philadelphia Metropolitan area to around 7 a.m. in the Lehigh Valley. Precipitation ended between 9 a.m. and 10 a.m. EST.In the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area in Bucks County a head-on two car crash on Pennsylvania State Route 309 caused a woman and child in both vehicles to be injured. In Montgomery County, an eight vehicle accident occurred on the Danenhauer Bridge in Norristown and closed the bridge for one hour. In Worcester Township, a man was seriously injured in a head-on collision. A number of serious accidents occurred on U.S. Route 422 including roll-overs and a vehicle that hit an embankment twice. All the major roadways around Philadelphia (the Blue Route, Interstate 95 and the Schuylkill Expressway) had accidents occurred. In Berks County, the eastbound lanes of Interstate 78 was closed in Tilden Township after a tractor-trailer jackknifed. In Douglass Township, Farmington Avenue was closed for several hours after a car driven by a 32-year-old male struck a small office building. In Northampton County, the westbound lanes of Interstate 78 was closed for one hour in Williams Township after a vehicle struck a disabled vehicle. In Forks Township, a vehicle drove into a home on Uhler Road. One lane of U.S. Route 22 was closed in Palmer Township because vehicles were sliding off the roadway. Several roads were closed because of icy conditions in Lehigh County. In the Poconos, several accidents occurred along U.S. Route 209 in Carbon County and about half a dozen accidents occurred along Interstates 80 and 380 in Monroe County.Accumulations included 4.1 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 3.8 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 3.3 inches in Springtown (Bucks County), 2.8 inches in Warrington and Sellersville (Bucks County), 2.5 inches in Boyertown and Hamburg (Berks County), Macungie (Lehigh County), Palm (Montgomery County) and Forks Township (Northampton County), 2.2 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 1.5 inches in Reading (Berks County) and Phoenixville (Chester County), 1.0 inch in Valley Forge (Chester County) and 0.6 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport. The greatest ice accretions (closer to two-tenths of an inch) occurred in the local Philadelphia area where the changeover occurred sooner. The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by a fast moving low pressure system that moved from Oklahoma on the morning of the 3rd to the Maryland panhandle at 7 a.m. EST on the morning of the 4th and was quickly offshore by that afternoon.

The combination of snow melt and the precipitation that fell from the afternoon of the15th into the early morning of the 16th caused minor flooding along the Wissahickon Creek. The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth was above its 5 foot flood stage from 827 a.m. EST through 303 p.m. EST on the 16th. It crested at 5.8 feet at 1230 p.m. EST. The Wissahickon Creek also flooded in neighboring Montgomery County. Precipitation totals included 1.21 inches at the Franklin Institute, 1.18 inches at Philadelphia International Airport and 1.14 inches at Northeast Philadelphia Airport. The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed on the Gulf Coast early on the 15th. A broad southerly flow brought moisture into the region. The low itself moved northeast through the Delaware Valley overnight on the 15th and was located near New York City at sunrise on the 16th. The track helped bring moisture from both the Atlantic Ocean and the Gulf of Mexico and dropped it across Philadelphia.

Thunderstorm winds tore the roof off a barn in Knoxville.

Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees in Duncansville.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees in Bellefonte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that developed along the Gulf Coast States on the 5th moved quickly northeast and dropped heavy snow across the local Philadelphia area with lighter snow in the northwestern Philadelphia suburbs. Little if any snow fell from Berks County and the Lehigh Valley northward. The snow began during the late afternoon and early evening of the 5th, but fell lightly through the evening. The heaviest snow fell after Midnight EST on the 6th. The snow ended around sunrise on the 6th. Accumulations averaged two to four inches with the highest amounts in the local Philadelphia area. Specific accumulations included 4.0 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and Havertown (Delaware County), 3.5 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County) and Neshaminy Falls (Bucks County), 2.8 inches in Unionville (Chester County), 2.5 inches in New Hope (Bucks County), 1.5 inches in Valley Forge (Chester County), 1.2 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County) and 0.5 inches in Royersford (Montgomery County), Fricks (Bucks County) and Glenmoore (Chester County)  A low pressure system developed over Georgia early on the 5th and moved northeast. The low deepened as it began to move off the coast of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina on Monday night the 5th. The low pressure system moved relatively quickly and was east of New England by noon on the 6th. The fast movement and the relative offshore track prevented heavy snow from getting much farther west than the local Philadelphia area.

Gusty northwest winds buffeted eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the first half of the day on the 10th. The northwest winds were caused by the difference in surface pressure between an intensifying low pressure system moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley in Canada and a high pressure system building east from the central Mississippi Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 43 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and 41 mph in Doylestown (Bucks County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and an associated cold front moved east across the region on December 1st.  Cold air filtered into Northwest Pennsylvania behind this front and caused lake effect snow showers to develop late in the afternoon of the 1st.  The snow remained light through the evening hours with accumulations generally less than an inch through midnight.  The snow intensfied during the morning hours of the 2nd.  Visibilities at times were less than one half mile with snowfall rates in excess of an inch per.  The snow tapered to flurries during the late evening hours of the 2nd and ended altogether early on the 3rd.   Snowfall totals across Northwest Pennsylvania were generally 8 to 16 inches inland from Lake Erie.  Accumulations near the shoreline were generally 4 to 8 inches.  A peak total of nearly 21 inches was reported at Corry (Erie County) with 20 inches reported near Meadville in Crawford County.

The combination of snow melt and the precipitation that fell from the afternoon of the15th into the early morning of the 16th caused minor flooding along a couple of creeks in Delaware County. Chester Creek in Chester was above its 8 foot flood stage from 451 a.m. EST through 740 a.m. EST on the 16th. It crested at 8.68 feet at 545 a.m. EST. Crum Creek at Newtown Square was above its 6 foot flood stage from 422 a.m. through 855 a.m. EST on the 16th. It crested at 7.48 feet at 7 a.m. EST. Precipitation totals included 1.85 inches at Crum Creek and 1.84 inches at Chadds Ford. The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed on the Gulf Coast early on the 15th. A broad southerly flow brought moisture into the region. The low itself moved northeast through the Delaware Valley overnight on the 15th and was located near New York City at sunrise on the 16th. The track helped bring moisture from both the Atlantic Ocean and the Gulf of Mexico and dropped it across Delaware County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of rain showers that moved through Reading blew out several windows at a vacant outlet building in Reading. The peak wind gust at the Reading Regional Airport was 37 mph.

Gusty northwest winds buffeted eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the first half of the day on the 10th. The northwest winds were caused by the difference in surface pressure between an intensifying low pressure system moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley in Canada and a high pressure system building east from the central Mississippi Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 43 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and 41 mph in Doylestown (Bucks County).

Light snow fell through the morning in the Poconos. Farther to the south in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley rain and snow began falling in the early afternoon. The air mass though was so unstable that heavier snow showers and even thundersnows occurred. This caused a quick burst of heavy snow around 3 p.m. EST. Even though air temperatures were above freezing, the heavy snow dropped temperatures to around 32 degrees and permitted the snow to accumulate on roadways. The snow ended by 5 p.m. EST in most places. A second batch of lighter snow fell later that evening. Accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches with the highest amounts in the Poconos. In Berks County, there were dozens of accidents between 130 p.m. EST and 430 p.m. EST. Vehicles slid down embankments and off roadways. In Perry Township,off of Pennsylvania State Route 61, a vehicle spun out of control into gas pumps. The driver was not seriously injured. In both Northampton and Monroe Counties driving conditions were described as horrible with numerous accidents. There were about 50 reported accidents in Northampton County, many of them occurred in the northern part of the county. Westbound U.S. Route 22 was snarled because of a serious single vehicle accident near Pennsylvania State Route 191. For the second time this month, a vehicle bearly skidded into the Lehigh River in Easton. A pair of head-on collisions with injuries occurred in Moore Township and Lower Mount Bethel Township. Specific accumulations included 3.0 inches in Tannersville (Monroe County), 2.5 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.4 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 2.2 inches in Macungie (Lehigh County), 1.5 inches in Nazareth (Northampton County), 1.3 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and Hellertown (Northampton County), 1.0 inch in Vinemont and Oley (Berks County) and 0.5 inches in Reading (Berks County). The heavier snow showers and thunder were triggered by the combination of a vigorous upper air disturbance and cold front that moved through the region.

The northwest flow around an intensifying northeaster brought strong winds to eastern Pennsylvania during the late morning and early afternoon on the 22nd. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 46 mph in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 44 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 41 mph in Reading (Berks County), 40 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 38 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport. The low pressure system responsible for the strong winds intensified from a 1007mb low near Jacksonville, Florida at 7 a.m. EST on the 21st, to a 996mb low near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 21st, to a 979mb low about 100 miles east of Atlantic City, New Jersey at 7 a.m. EST on the 22nd, to a 972mb low near Boston, Massachusetts at 1 p.m. EST on the 22nd to a 968mb low in northeastern Maine at 7p.m. EST on the 22nd. The strongest winds occurred as the intensifying low pressure system moved through southern New England.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow started around noon on the 24th. By 10 PM, West Hickory accumulated 6 inches.

An Alberta Clipped passed to the north of Lake Erie during the evening hours of November 23rd.  An arctic cold front trailing this low swept east across Northwest Pennsylvania early on the 24th.  Cold northwest winds behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to develop just before daybreak on the 24th.  These bands quickly intensified and by mid-morning, visibilities in some areas were less than one quarter mile.   Northwest winds gusting in excess of 30 mph accompanied the snow and caused considerable blowing and drifting.  The snow showers tappered to flurries during the early evening hours.  Snowfall totals of 8 to 16 inches were reported in both Crawford and inland Erie Counties by sunset on the 24th.  Then, after midnight on the 25th, an upper level disturbance rotated through the region.  This caused a new round of lake effect snow showers to develop.  This activity diminished during the afternoon of the 25th after another 6 to 9 inches of snow had fallen.  Two day totals for this event were 12 to 18 inches in many locations.  A peak of 23.5 inches was measured at Corry (Erie County) with 19 inches at Meadville  (Crawford County).

The combination of being near the center of a high pressure system that helped produce light winds and a deep snowpack caused record or near record breaking low temperatures on the morning of the 14th from the Lehigh Valley northward. For many this was the coldest day of the meteorological winter season. The low of 4 degrees below zero in East Stroudsburg tied the record for the day. The low of 2 degrees below zero at the Lehigh Valley International Airport established a new record low for the day. Six people in Allentown (Lehigh County) were sickened by carbon monoxide emissions from their faulty heating unit. The cold weather caused an increase in demand for electricity and Pennsylvania Power and Light came within 100 megawatts (7,174) between 7 and 8 a.m. EST of tying their winter hourly megawatt usage record set in December 20, 2004.

The combination of snow melt and the precipitation that fell from the afternoon of the15th into the early morning of the 16th caused minor flooding along the Neshaminy Creek in Bucks County. The Neshaminy Creek at Langhorne was above its 9 foot flood stage from 1026 a.m. EST through 401 p.m. EST on the 16th. It crested at 9.35 feet at 145 p.m. EST.  Precipitation totals included 1.25 inches in Southampton and Sellersville, 1.21 inches in Perkasie, 1.17 inches in Neshaminy Falls and 1.16 inches in Springtown. The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed on the Gulf Coast early on the 15th. A broad southerly flow brought moisture into the region. The low itself moved northeast through the Delaware Valley overnight on the 15th and was located near New York City at sunrise on the 16th. The track helped bring moisture from both the Atlantic Ocean and the Gulf of Mexico and dropped it across Bucks County.
